“You’re new here.”

“Hm.” The Anat taps a clawed finger against the counter, his bright eyes narrowing. He seems to be examining you, or rather your gear, quite closely. “I see... Do you have a name?”
Despite the locale, you get the impression the stranger means you no ill will. “[pc.name] Steele. And you are?”.

You stay silent for a moment, trying to size up the Anat. He’s armed, but isn’t making any threatening moves. “[pc.name] Steele. You?”

The Anat’s eyes give a slight twitch, a sign of recognition. “Steele... interesting. I am Vulriks, and I am at your service.” He bows his head slightly, but keeps his piercing eyes on you. “If you, the great [pc.name] Steele, have anything you would like to sell, I would be most honored.”

You look around the room once more at the containers littering the room. “You’re a merchant?” A few of the open containers are loaded full with various, mostly dubiously legal, odds and ends. You turn back to face Vulriks. “What do you sell?”

The Anat scoffs and waves a single clawed hand in the air, as if shooing away the question. “I do not sell my stock. Not here at least. What is it the Rat said... something about how I do not ‘shit where I eat.’ The expression is lost on me, but I think I understand her meaning. I keep my business here focused.”
“If you wish to sell, I will buy. And believe me, I will buy whatever it is you would wish to sell.” Vulriks shifts in place, his bright eyes suddenly round and kind. “So, shall we do business, Per?”

Like the rest of his kind, his face is covered by a natural mask, a plate of bone-white material, covered with intricate etchings. He keeps his hands on the counter, his flesh rough and dark red, both capped with four white, predatory claws, similar to his mask in composition. His lower half is harder to judge. You can tell he has two digitigrade legs concealed by his cloak, and when he shifts his weight you hear a pair of heavy boots scuff the floor. You eyeball a particularly interesting-looking shoulder mounted rocket launcher sticking out from an open container. Now you can’t help but ask. “So where exactly do I have to go to buy this stuff then?”

Vulriks lifts a single finger and waves it in the air. “Tsk, tsk. That I cannot tell you. My clients would be very cross with me if I gave away those particular locations. But I will tell you what...” his blue eyes narrow, and you suspect behind his mask he’s smiling. “If I ever come across something that I think you simply must own, I will hold it for you. Deal?”
You suppose that’s the best you’re going to get...
“Oh.” Despite understanding his position, you still feel the urge to inquire if he thinks that will change.
Vulriks laughs, the sound only slightly muffled by his mask. “Tell you what, Per. Deal with me, and we can talk about whatever you want. Let’s think of it as ‘The price to do business’. Deal?”
Sounds reasonable enough.
Talk
Vulriks chuckles, seemingly amused by your question. “I promise you, my friend. I sell everything I buy. Just not under the same roof.”
“Everything?” You turn to regard one of the open containers, regarding a pile of worn out junk, mostly old ship parts, sticking out from the top. “How exactly do you manage that?”

From behind his mask you hear something like lips smacking, but you’re not sure what’s exactly going on behind the damned thing. “I will admit, some things stick around much longer than others. But if I’ve learned one thing in this line of work it’s this: trash and treasure are subjective”
“I think the expression is ‘One man’s trash is another man’s treasure’.”

Vulriks laughs, smacking the counter in a sign of approval. “I like that! Yes! That’s good. Very true. Now, I will admit sometimes, ehh...” Vulriks positions his hand sideways in the air, wiggling it. “I take a loss on something. But I am not worried about profits.”
Vulriks sighs, his blue eyes rolling behind his mask. “I have heard that before.”
Vulriks taps his finger, nodding slightly. “Not many understand my position.”

He continues. “In the Empire, everything we do is for the greater good. Even still, I adhere to that principle; despite my current, situation.” With an outstretched arm, he motions around the room. “Everything I do, all of my dealings, I do for my greater good. For my people, and my Empire.”
You chew on his words for a moment. “How does trading with pirates and selling contraband help your people?”

Vulriks groans slightly, his claws tapping on the counter. “No. That’s not quite right. As far as they know I am still very much present, albeit preoccupied.” The Anat stops his digits from tapping, sighing from behind his mask. “I was to die, you see. Sentenced to a lifetime breaking rocks as punishment for my crimes.”
“Crimes?” You look the tall man up and down.

“Ehh. It was what it was. I managed to escape, clearly, and chose to leave the Empire,” his tone is somber, almost sad. “I keep in contact with some friends I met during my time imprisoned. They chose to stay, despite the possible consequences. They are still to this day my most lucrative customers.”
“Wait...” You look at the containers around you, thinking on some of the contents you’ve seen sticking out. “You move contraband into the Empire?”

It is the greatest service I can provide for them, despite my current situation.” Vulriks seems content, and if you had to guess you’d say behind his mask he must be smiling.

Vulriks doesn’t answer at first, his blue eyes narrowing behind his mask. “Ehh, a topic for another time. Another tale that needs, the proper... context. Did you have something else you wanted to discuss?”
Vulriks rolls his eyes. “Where else could I conduct my business? When dealing with pirates, one must be where the pirates are.”
“Fair enough...” you cede the point, but remain unsatisfied with this answer.

As if reading your mind, Vulriks offers some clarification. “I realize this place is rather... ehh...” he searches for the correct word a moment, his blue eyes wandering. “Unstable. But it’s the best for my needs. Sure, I live under the constant threat of being stabbed and robbed, but I fear for the fate of the poor soul who does this to me.”
You eyeball the blaster on his hip. “You must think pretty highly of yourself.”

“Where to begin? Well my life started with my birth on New Texas. My mother had me before she had to get the Treatment. She... I don’t talk to her anymore. It’s like looking at a ghost, a very stupid ghost.” Vivi seems irritated. “Anyway I went to school like most kids do. I didn’t have a lot of friends, but I had a knack for science: specifically chemistry. My life was great until the time came for my mom to take the Treatment. We weren’t poor, per se, but couldn’t afford to get off the planet, so she had no choice. We both knew it would change her, but I never could have imagined just how extreme it would be. The day it happened, when she came home... she didn’t come home alone.” She glances away. “It... No one should see their family like that, doing those things with strangers, their mind so totally melted that they think that it’s ok... I’m sorry.” She takes a moment to wipe the tears forming at her eyes. “Anyway I ran away from home. I spent the next few years wandering from place to place, living in the wilds, hiding from authorities, and living off of the kindness of the few willing to shelter me. My luck ran out when I finally managed to get myself off the planet. The ship I was on got hit by pirates, and they found me hiding in the storage. I was able to demonstrate my chemistry skills by detonating the supplies I had. It knocked me and about half of the pirates out. Probably not my smartest decision, but it worked out. The leader was impressed enough with my quick thinking to have me sent here to make drugs for the station. It’s better than dying, or the other awful things they do to people.”

I have a few reliable customers — I can’t tell you who they are, but you can probably figure it out yourself if you want. Anyway, I live a decently comfortable life here, at least compared to the miners. So yeah, I’m fine... not great, but fine. Would love to leave if I could.”\n\t
